15: The summary of David's reign: wayyimlōk dāwiḏ ʿal kol yiśrāʾēl wayhî dāwiḏ ʿōśeh mišpāṭ ûṣᵉdāqāh lᵉḵol ʿammô — 'David reigned over all Israel and David administered justice (mišpāṭ) and righteousness (ṣᵉdāqāh) to all his people.' The paired nouns mišpāṭ ûṣᵉdāqāh constitute the OT's standard formulation of the royal ideal: the king's obligation to administer the covenantal legal order (mišpāṭ) and the relational right-standing that supports it (ṣᵉdāqāh). This pair appears in the Davidic Psalms' royal ideal (Psalms 72:1-2: 'Give the king your mišpāṭîm, O God, and your ṣᵉdāqāh to the royal son'), in Isaiah's messianic oracle (Isaiah 9:7: 'to uphold it with mišpāṭ and with ṣᵉdāqāh'), and in Jeremiah's messianic title (Jeremiah 23:5: 'the Branch of righteousness who shall execute mišpāṭ and ṣᵉdāqāh'). The formula in 2 Samuel 8:15 is not merely administrative summary but the theological claim that David's reign embodied the royal ideal that will find its perfect realization in the Davidic Messiah whose justice will be universal and eternal.
1: Vayehi acharei-chen vayak David et-Pelishtim vayakhnieim (“after this David struck the Philistines and subdued them”). The formula acharei-chen (after this/these things) is the standard transition phrase introducing a new campaign narrative (cf. 2 Samuel 10:1; 2 Samuel 13:1). The verb nakah (struck down) paired with kana’ (hiphil: subdued/humbled) is the typical Deuteronomistic victory vocabulary. Metheg-ammah is a hapax of uncertain identification; the parallel in 1 Chronicles 18:1 reads “Gath and its surrounding towns,” suggesting this was a key Philistine center. The chapter serves as a summary of David’s military reach at the peak of his power.
2: The Moabite subjugation: David made them lie on the ground and measured with a cord (veyemaddem bacchevel), killing two lengths and sparing one full length. This measuring-for-death is unique in the Hebrew Bible — no other passage describes exactly this procedure. The Moabite tribute (lamas nose’ minchah, bearing tribute) uses the standard ANE tribute formulas. The severity toward Moab is unexplained in the text (Ruth was a Moabite; David stored his parents there, 1 Samuel 22:3-4). The narrative simply records the conquest without commentary, as is characteristic of the victory catalog genre.
3: Hadadezer (Hadad-ezer, “Hadad is a help”) son of Rehob, king of Zobah — the Aramean kingdom north of Damascus. David struck him down belokho lehashiv yado be-nahar Perath (“as Hadadezer was on his way to reassert his control at the Euphrates”). The verb hashiv yadо (restore/reassert his hand) implies Hadadezer was attempting to reextend his northern sphere of influence. The Euphrates boundary marks the theoretical extent of Israel’s covenanted territory (Genesis 15:18; Deuteronomy 1:7; 1 Kings 4:21) — David’s campaign fulfills the Abrahamic territorial promise.
4: David captured from Hadadezer 1,700 horsemen (parashim) and 20,000 foot soldiers (ragli). He hamstrung (vayeaqer) all the chariot horses (rekhev) except enough for 100 chariots (mea-rekhev hissir lo). Aqar (hamstring, piel) was the prescribed treatment for captured chariot horses in Deuteronomy 17:16 and Joshua 11:6, 9 — the Mosaic king legislation prohibited accumulation of horses, a symbol of military self-reliance. Retaining enough for 100 chariots while destroying the rest shows David maintaining the Mosaic constraint even while conducting imperial expansion. 1 Chronicles 18:4 records 7,000 horsemen — a textual variant.
5: The Syrians of Damascus (Aram Dammeseq) came to help Hadadezer; David struck down 22,000 of them. Damascus is mentioned here for the first time in the David narrative. The rescue coalition fails: David defeats the coalition members separately (cf. the Ammonite-Aramean coalition in 2 Samuel 10). The number 22,000 is a large round number characteristic of victory tallies in the ANE; such figures function rhetorically to communicate overwhelming victory rather than as precise census counts.
6: David placed garrisons (nitzavim, from natzav, stand/station) in Aramean Damascus, and the Syrians became vassal-tributaries (vayehi Aram David la-avadim nose’ei minchah, “and Aram became David’s servants bearing tribute”). The repeated refrain: vayoshea YHWH et-David bekhol asher halakh (“the LORD preserved/saved David wherever he went”). This divine-preservation formula appears twice (2 Samuel 8:6, 14) as a bracket around the campaign summary, interpreting all of David’s military success as YHWH’s work rather than David’s military skill.
7: David took the gold shields (shiltei ha-zahav) carried by Hadadezer’s officers and brought them to Jerusalem. Shelet (shield/quiver, also rendered “shield cases”) appears in the NT era as a loan-word in Greek; the gold shields are trophies of victory dedicated to YHWH rather than retained as personal wealth. The pattern of dedicating military plunder to YHWH’s treasury is the same as Samuel’s demand that Agag be devoted and the Amalekite cattle consecrated (1 Samuel 15:3) — the principle of cherem (devotion to YHWH) applied to foreign wealth.
8: From Betah (Tebah in 1 Chronicles 18:8) and Berothai, cities of Hadadezer, David took a very large quantity of bronze (nechashet harbeh me’od). Bronze was the primary metal for weapons and construction in this period before iron technology became dominant. Solomon will use this bronze for the temple furnishings (1 Kings 7:13-47; cf. 1 Chronicles 18:8 explicitly connects this bronze to the temple). David’s wars thus provision the temple David is not permitted to build — a transfer of war-spoil into sacred architecture mediated by a generation.
9: Toi (Tou in 1 Chronicles 18:9), king of Hamath, heard that David had destroyed the whole army of Hadadezer (hikah David et kol tzeva Hadad-ezer). Hamath was a significant Aramean city-state on the Orontes River, north of Zobah. Since Hadadezer had been Toi’s enemy (ki ish milchamot Tou hayah Hadad-ezer), David’s victory serves Hamath’s interests. The political intelligence Toi demonstrates — recognizing the new dominant power and sending an immediate diplomatic mission — is the standard response of smaller kingdoms to rising empires throughout the ANE.
10: Toi sent his son Joram (Yehoram in 1 Chronicles 18:10, a theophoric form) to David to extend greetings and congratulate him (leshalem lo ulevarkho, “to greet him and bless him”). Shalem (piel: establish peace, complete a formal greeting) is the root of shalom. Joram brought vessels of silver, gold, and bronze (keli keseph u-khle zahav u-khle nechoshet) — the standard diplomatic gift categories. The name Joram (“YHWH is exalted”) is ironic for a son of a Aramean king who worships Hadad; it may reflect the family’s political accommodation to Israelite power.
11: David dedicated these (gam otam hiqdish ha-melekh David laYHWH) to YHWH along with all the silver and gold he had set apart (hivdil) from the nations he had conquered. The verb hiqdish (consecrate/dedicate, hiphil of qadash) converts military booty into sacred donation. The verb hivdil (separate out) is used for the priestly act of distinguishing holy from common (Leviticus 10:10). David’s military treasury is systematically transferred to YHWH’s domain — the spoils of empire become the endowment for the temple.
12: The enumeration of conquered nations: Edom, Moab, Ammon, Philistines, Amalek, and the plunder from Hadadezer. Six nations/peoples appear in this tribute catalog. The list partially fulfills the enemies specified in YHWH’s commission to Israel (Deuteronomy 7:1: the seven nations; the Amalekites as special enemies, Exodus 17:16; Deuteronomy 25:17-19). Amalek is mentioned last — the perpetual enemy whose subjugation was commanded before the monarchy began (1 Sam 15). The completeness of the list signals the fulfillment of covenantal promises.
13: David made a name (vaya’as David shem) when he returned from killing Edomites in the Valley of Salt (Gei ha-Melach). Shem (name, reputation) is the reward of great deeds. The Valley of Salt is identified with a saltflat south of the Dead Sea. The number 18,000 Edomites killed is a round figure; 1 Chronicles 18:12 attributes the victory to Abishai and records the same number. The discrepancy may reflect different aspects of the same campaign or a scribal harmonization issue. What matters theologically is the subjugation of Edom (descendant of Esau), fulfilling the oracle of Genesis 25:23.
14: Garrisons throughout Edom (vayasem be-Edom nitzavim bekhol Edom) and all Edomites became David’s subjects (vayehi khol-Edom avadim leDavid). The repetition of the formula vayoshea YHWH et-David bekhol asher halakh (“the LORD preserved David wherever he went”) closes the victory catalog as it opened it (2 Samuel 8:6). The structural bracket is interpretive: the conquest is YHWH’s work, not David’s military genius. The fulfilled oracle of Genesis 27:40 (Esau will serve Jacob) is the covenantal framework for the Edom subjugation.
16: The administrative list (reshimat ha-paqqidim): Joab (Yo’av) son of Zeruiah over the army (al-ha-tzava); Jehoshaphat son of Ahilud as recorder (mazkir). The mazkir (one who records/brings to memory) was a high court official in Egyptian and Israelite administration, possibly the official responsible for presenting reports and recording royal decisions. The list follows the pattern of Egyptian and Mesopotamian administrative rosters that accompanied victory catalogs. A similar list appears in 2 Samuel 20:23-26, reflecting an expanded administration later in David’s reign.
17: Zadok (Tzadoq) son of Ahitub and Ahimelech son of Abiathar as priests (kohanim). The dual-priest arrangement is significant: Zadok is an Aaronide from the line that will eventually displace Abiathar’s Elide line (1 Kings 2:26-27). Seraiah (Seraya) as secretary (sofer). The sofer (scribe/secretary) was a key administrative official responsible for correspondence and legal documents (cf. 2 Kings 12:10; 2 Samuel 18:18). The list of officials mirrors known ANE palace administrative structures.
18: Benaiah (Benayahu) son of Jehoiada over the Cherethites and Pelethites (ha-Keretî ve-ha-Peletî). The Cherethites (possibly Cretans or Philistines) and Pelethites were David’s elite mercenary bodyguard — non-Israelite professional soldiers loyal to the king personally rather than to tribal or national interests. Their presence in David’s court reflects the cosmopolitan nature of his administration. Uvnei David kohanim hayu (“and David’s sons served as royal priests”). The priestly function of the king’s sons is unusual; 1 Chronicles 18:17 replaces kohanim with rishonim le-yad ha-melekh (chief officials at the king’s side), perhaps softening the priestly implication.
